1: imrei ('emer, H561) — the plural noun for ‘words, sayings’; distinct from dabar (the weightier spoken word or event). The father’s instruction is characterized as 'emer — the lighter communicative word — yet these words are to be “stored up” (tzaphan, to hide/treasure, used of hiding valuables). The contrast between the casual word-form and the precious storage-command creates the tone of the entire chapter.
2: ke-'ishon 'einecha — “as the 'ishon of your eye.” 'Ishon (H380) means ‘pupil of the eye’ but literally translates as ‘the little man’ — the tiny reflection of oneself visible in another’s pupil. The idiom of guarding like the pupil of the eye appears in Deuteronomy 32:10 and Psalms 17:8 of YHWH’s care for Israel; here the father uses it of the son’s care for the father’s teaching. The most reflexively sensitive organ of the body.
3: qashor 'al-'etzbe'otecha — bind on your fingers. 'Etzba' (H676) is the finger, the instrument of pointing and precision. The binding of instruction on the fingers extends the Shema-metaphor (Deuteronomy 6:8: bind on your hand) to a new context: the father’s teaching is to be as constantly present as one’s own fingers. The physical, embodied quality of this metaphor reinforces the MKT’s anthropology of instruction as bodily formation.
4:
'achoti — “my sister.” 'Achot (H269) as a term of intimate address for wisdom is significant: in Song of Songs the beloved calls the bride “my sister” (Proverbs 4:9-10, 12; Proverbs 5:1-2), suggesting an erotic register of intimacy that is deliberately activated here to counter the seductive address of the adulteress in vv16-18. The father invites the son to the most intimate possible relationship with wisdom as the competing attraction to the “foreign woman” ('ishah zarah).

moda' — “close companion, kinsman.” Moda' (cf. Ruth 2:1, Proverbs 3:2) is a term for the closest family member; the parallelism with “sister” intensifies the intimacy of the wisdom-relationship the father recommends.
5: tishmereka me-'ishah zarah — “she [wisdom] will guard you from the foreign/strange woman.” 'Ishah zarah (strange/foreign woman) is the dominant characterization of the seductress throughout Proverbs 1-9. Zarah (H2114) means ‘alien, strange, foreign’ — not necessarily a non-Israelite, but one outside the legitimate covenant bond. The nokhriyah (‘alien woman’) in the parallel is a near-synonym emphasizing her outsider status relative to the father’s household and instruction.
6: be-'ad halloni — “through my window.” Chalon (H2474) is the standard window term; here the father positions himself as observer rather than participant. The narrative frame of vv6-23 is the father’s eyewitness account (“I saw”) — the literary device of the reported observation lends the cautionary tale the authority of testimony. The 'eshnav (H822, v6b, ‘lattice/casement’) refers to a window with a screening lattice.
7: va-'er ba-peta'im — “I discerned among the simple ones.” Bein (H995) is the verb of discernment/understanding used extensively in wisdom literature; here it is the father’s act of distinguishing. Peta'im (the simple, the naïve) are the category of the morally unformed who have not yet chosen wisdom or folly — vulnerable in both directions. The object of the father’s discernment is a na'ar chaser-lev — “a young man lacking heart (= mind, moral sense).” Lev (heart) in Hebrew denotes the seat of thought, will, and moral judgment, not merely emotion.
8: qoreh le-fathe'ach beithah — “passing near the corner of her street, drawing near the entrance of her house.” The young man’s path description (“in the street”, “in the square”, “near the corner”, “the way to her house”) is the geography of increasingly deliberate proximity. He is not yet at her door, but he is approaching it step by step — a wisdom observation about the incremental nature of moral failure.
9: be-'ishon layelah ve-'afelah — “in the darkness of night and the thick darkness.” 'Ishon layelah reuses the 'ishon word from v2 (the pupil of the eye), creating an ironic wordplay: the “little man of night” (the pupil — the organ for guarding wisdom) is now the metaphor for the deepest darkness. The darkness setting establishes the moral coding: the events of vv10-23 are hidden from public accountability, the shame-preventing community oversight of Israelite life.
10: 'eshet qedeshah — the MKT follows the note that 'ishah here does not require the explicit translation “prostitute” (KJV) but rather emphasizes the guarded heart (netzurat lev). Natsar (to guard, watch, keep) applied to the heart is the wisdom vocabulary of deliberate concealment of intention — she knows what she is doing. The phrase “dressed as a harlot” (shith zonah) characterizes her appearance rather than her legal status; she may be an adulteress (cf. v19) rather than a professional prostitute.
11: homiyah hi' ve-soreref — “she is noisy and wayward.” Hamah (H1993, to be noisy, to clamor) is used of tumultuous crowds and boiling water; the seductress’s characteristic is her disruptive presence. Sorer (H5637) means ‘wayward, stubborn’ — the same root used of the “stubborn and rebellious son” in Deuteronomy 21:18-21. The contrast with Wisdom’s structured invitation (Proverbs 8-9) is deliberate: Wisdom calls from the heights; Folly/the seductress lurks in the streets.
12: 'atah ba-chutz 'atah ba-rechovot — “now outside, now in the streets, lurking at every corner.” The omnipresence of the seductress in the public spaces of the city is part of her characterization: she is the antithesis of the domestic virtue of Proverbs 31:10-31. The three-fold location description (outside, streets, corners) emphasizes her unstable and deliberately exposing presence.
13: ve-hecheziqah bo ve-nashah lo — “she seized him and kissed him.” The grammatical subject of the verbs is the woman, making her the agent of the pursuit: she grabs him, she kisses him. The reversal of the expected courtship dynamic (male pursuit of female) is a deliberate element of the characterization of the seductress as one who transgresses social roles. he'ezah paneyha — “with a brazen face”; az panim is used in Proverbs 21:29 of the wicked man — the same characterization is applied here to the seductress.
14: zevachim shelamim 'alai — “peace offerings are with me; today I have paid my vows.” The shelamim (peace/fellowship offering, H8002) is the category of sacrifice where the worshipper kept a portion of the meat for communal eating after the priestly portions were given. Leviticus 7:16-17 required this meat to be eaten within one or two days — so her statement “today I have paid my vows” means she has abundant fresh meat available. The seduction uses the sacrificial calendar opportunistically: religious observance becomes a pretext for immorality.
15: yatzati liqra'techa leshacher panecha — “I came out to meet you, to seek your face earnestly.” The verb shachar (H7836, to seek earnestly, to seek at dawn) is used in wisdom literature for the diligent pursuit of wisdom itself (Proverbs 8:17: “those who seek me earnestly will find me”). The seductress deliberately mimics the vocabulary of wisdom-seeking — the verbal echo is ironic and pedagogically intentional.
16: 'erez 'arashti mitati divedim 'atun mitzrayim — “I have spread my bed with colored blankets and fine linen from Egypt.” 'Erekh (to spread/arrange, used of setting out a banquet or preparing a battle array) and the Egyptian linen ('etun mitzrayim) invoke luxury and sophistication: these are imported goods from the dominant cultural power of the day. The material detail locates the seduction in the context of international trade and cosmopolitan wealth, making her offer appear as participation in the broader world of cultured life.
17: naddafti mishkavi mor 'ahalim ve-qinamon — “I have scented my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.” All three spices are luxury imports used in the Song of Songs (Song of Solomon 3:6; Proverbs 4:6, 14) as erotic fragrances. The seductress deliberately invokes the vocabulary and sensory world of legitimate marital love (the Song) to package her adulterous invitation in the most attractive possible form. Mor (myrrh, H4753), 'ahalim (aloes, H174), and qinamon (cinnamon, H7076) together represent the full spectrum of imported aromatic luxury.
18: nerve'eh dodim 'ad-habo'ker — “let us drink our fill of love until morning.” Ravah (to drink one’s fill, to be saturated) is used of saturation with water; here it is applied to sexual satisfaction. Dodim (H1730) means ‘love-making, caresses’ — the term is used throughout Song of Songs for physical love. The time-frame “until morning” expresses the total availability of the offer while presupposing the husband’s extended absence (v19).
19: ki ein ha-'ish be-beito — “for the man is not in his house.” The detail of the husband’s absence is the structural hinge of the seduction: she has knowledge of his travel plans and has prepared accordingly. The Hebrew ha-'ish (lit. ‘the man’, using the definite article — ‘her husband’) makes the adulterous context explicit. She is not a widow or a single woman; she is using her husband’s absence as an opportunity.
20: tzror ha-kesef laqach be-yado — “he has taken the money bag with him.” The husband carries the family wealth, suggesting a prosperous household: the seductress lives in luxury (cf. v16-17) and her husband is a merchant of means. The detail be-yom ha-kese' yavo' beito — “at the full moon he will return” — uses kese' (H3677, full moon) to specify the extended absence. A full lunar cycle gives her a calendar-window for the affair.
21: hittatu be-rov liqchah — “she seduced him with her many speeches.” Natah (to turn, to incline) with the sense of seduction: she turned his path. Chelqat sefateyha — “by the smooth talk of her lips.” Cheleq (H2506) means ‘portion, smoothness, flattery’; the same term is used in v5 for the words the seductress speaks. The smooth lips that deceive are the inverse of Wisdom’s straight and righteous speech (Proverbs 8:8-9: “all the words of my mouth are righteous; there is nothing crooked or perverse in them”).
22: holech 'achareyha pith'om ke-shor el-tevach yavo' — “he followed her at once, like an ox going to slaughter.” Pith'om (suddenly, immediately) is significant: the seduction works on the young man without deliberation or resistance. The ox-to-slaughter and fool-to-punishment comparisons frame the seduced young man’s action as both his own culpability ('evil, H191 = ‘fool’) and his victimhood. The animal comparisons use the OT’s standard imagery of the thoughtless victim.
23: 'ad yefallach chetz kevedo — “until an arrow pierces his liver.” The liver (kaved, H3516) in Hebrew thought is the seat of deepest inward experience (cf. Lamentations 2:11: “my liver is poured out”). An arrow through the liver is a mortal wound; the metaphor completes the animal-slaughter imagery of v22: this is not merely social embarrassment but death. The transition from “pleasant-seeming” to “lethal” without warning is the wisdom point.
24: ushme'u-li banim — “and now, sons, listen to me.” The father returns from narrative to direct address; the observation of vv6-23 is now applied to the sons as direct warning. The plural “sons” (also in Proverbs 5:7, Proverbs 8:32) suggests the discourse is not merely private fatherly instruction but a formative teaching document for a wider group of students in the wisdom tradition.
25: 'al-yeshte 'el-derakheyha libecha — “do not let your heart be drawn to her ways.” The danger is not primarily physical proximity but internal orientation: natah (to turn, incline) the heart is the first movement toward disaster. The heart (lev) as the decision-making center means that the internal posture precedes and determines the external action. Wisdom addresses the root, not only the symptom.
26: ki-rabbim challelim hipilah — “for many are those she has brought low.” The plural and the quantifier “many” (rabbim) extend the seductress beyond one dangerous woman to a pattern: she represents a type of death-inviting path that has claimed many victims. Challal (H2491) means ‘pierced, slain’ — using the vocabulary of battlefield death for the fate of adultery’s victims. The “mighty ones” ('atzumim, H6099) who are among her slain emphasizes that strength is no defense against this form of death.
27: darkhei sheol beithah — “her house is the roads of Sheol.” The plural darkei sheol (roads/ways of Sheol) is distinctive: the adulteress’s house is not a single path downward but a network of paths all leading to Sheol. The final image of “the inner chambers of death” (chadrei mavet) develops the domestic metaphor of the house: she has invited him into her house (vv11-18), but her house is a house of death. The structural inversion of wisdom’s house (Proverbs 9:1: seven pillars, a prepared feast) is complete: the adulteress’s house leads to Sheol, wisdom’s house leads to life (Proverbs 9:6).
